subject: Physics
q: A bird is flying in a direction 30° East of North with a speed of 5 kmh−1 and a cyclist is going on the road towards East at a speed of 10 kmh−1. The velocity of bird observed by cyclist is
question: A bird is flying in a direction 30° East of North with a speed of 5 kmh−1 and a cyclist is going on the road towards East at a speed of 10 kmh−1. The velocity of bird observed by cyclist is
options: 5√3 kmh−1 in a direction at 60° West of North
5√3 kmh−1 in a direction at 30° West of North
solution:
The cyclist moves directly towards the East at a speed of 10 km/h. Therefore, their velocity vector is:

$$\vec{v}_c = 10\hat{\imath}\,\text{km/h}$$

The bird flies with a speed of 5 km/h at an angle of $30^\circ$ East of North.

Resolving this into components:

The component along the East (x-axis) is $v_b\sin(30^\circ)$.

The component along the North (y-axis) is $v_b\cos(30^\circ)$.

The velocity vector of bird is,

$$\vec{v}_b = 5\sin(30^\circ)\,\hat{i} + 5\cos(30^\circ)\,\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{v}_b = 5\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\hat{i} + 5\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\hat{j} = 2.5\hat{i} + \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}\hat{j}$$

The relative velocity vector of bird with respect to the cyclist is:

$$\vec{v}_{b/c} = \vec{v}_b - \vec{v}_c$$

$$\vec{v}_{b/c} = \left( 2.5\hat{\imath} + \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}\hat{\jmath} \right) - (10\hat{\imath})$$

$$\vec{v}_{b/c} = (2.5 - 10)\hat{\imath} + \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}\hat{\jmath}$$

$$\vec{v}_{b/c} = -7.5\hat{\imath} + \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}\hat{\jmath} = -\frac{15}{2}\hat{\imath} + \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}\hat{\jmath}$$

Magnitude of relative velocity of bird with respect to cyclist is:

$$|\vec{v}_{b/c}| = \sqrt{\left(-\frac{15}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\frac{225}{4} + 25 \times \frac{3}{4}}$$

$$|\vec{v}_{b/c}| = \sqrt{\frac{225 + 75}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{300}{4}} = \sqrt{75} = \sqrt{25 \times 3} = 5\sqrt{3}\ \text{km/h}$$

$$\tan \theta = \frac{\frac{15}{2}}{\frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{15}{5\sqrt{3}} = \sqrt{3}$$

$$\theta = 60^\circ$$

Since this angle is measured from North heading towards the West, the direction is $60^\circ$ West of North.

Therefore, the velocity of the bird observed by the cyclist is $5\sqrt{3}\ \text{kmh}^{-1}$ in a direction at $60^\circ$ West of North.

Thus, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: Two ships are 10 km apart on a line running from south to north. The one farther north is streaming towards west at 20 km hrâˆ’1. The other is streaming north at 20 km hrâˆ’1. The distance of closest approach is
question: Two ships are 10 km apart on a line running from south to north. The one farther north is streaming towards west at 20 km hrâˆ’1. The other is streaming north at 20 km hrâˆ’1. The distance of closest approach is
solution:
Let's use a coordinate system where North is the positive y-axis and East is the positive x-axis.

Ship 1 (farther north) is initially at $\mathrm{A}(0,10)$. It moves West.

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_1=-201 \hat{\mathrm{~km}} / \mathrm{h} $$

Ship 2 (South) is initially at $\mathrm{B}(0,0)$. It moves North.

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_2=20 \hat{\jmath} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h} $$

The relative velocity of Ship 2 relative to Ship 1 is,

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{21}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_2-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_1 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{21}=(20 \hat{\jmath})-(-20\hat{i})$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{21}=20 \hat{1}+20 \hat{\jmath} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h} $$

The magnitude of relative velocity is,

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rel}}=\sqrt{20^2+20^2}=20 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h} $$

The relative velocity vector makes an angle $\theta$ with the x -axis:

$$ \tan \theta=\frac{20}{20}=1 \Rightarrow \theta=45^{\circ} $$

The distance of closest approach $\left(\mathrm{d}_{\min }\right)$ is the length of the perpendicular drawn from the stationary Ship $1(\mathrm{~A})$ to the relative path of Ship 2.

In the right-angled triangle formed by the perpendicular :

$$ \frac{\mathrm{d}_{\min }}{\mathrm{AB}}=\sin 45^{\circ} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{d}_{\min }=10 \cdot \sin 45^{\circ}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{d}_{\min }=10 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{d}_{\min }=5 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~km} $$

Therefore, the distance of closest approach is $5 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~km}$.

Hence, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: A helicopter is flying south with a speed of 50 kmhâˆ’1. A train is moving with the same speed towards east. The relative velocity of the helicopter as seen by the passengers in the train will be towards :
question: A helicopter is flying south with a speed of 50 kmhâˆ’1. A train is moving with the same speed towards east. The relative velocity of the helicopter as seen by the passengers in the train will be towards :
solution:
Let the unit vector pointing east be denoted as Ã® and the unit vector pointing north be $\hat{\mathrm{j}}$. So, west is $-\hat{\mathrm{i}}$ and south is $-\hat{\jmath}$.

The helicopter travels due South at a speed of $50 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. The velocity of the helicopter $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{H}}\right)$ is,

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{H}}=0 \hat{\imath}-50 \hat{\jmath}=-50 \hat{\jmath} $$

The train travels due East at the same speed of $50 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. The velocity of train is,

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{T}}=50 \hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}=50 \hat{\imath}$

The relative velocity of the helicopter as seen by the passengers inside the moving train,

$$\vec{V}_{H/T} = \vec{V}_H - \vec{V}_T$$

$$\vec{V}_{H/T} = (-50\hat{j}) - (50\hat{i})$$

$$\vec{V}_{H/T} = -50\hat{i} - 50\hat{j}$$

The horizontal component is $-50\hat{i}$, which points in the west direction.

The vertical component is $-50\hat{j}$, which points in the south direction.

Let he direction angle $\theta$ with respect to the west axis is given by:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{50}{50} = 1 \rightarrow \theta = 45^{\circ}$$

Therefore, the relative velocity of the helicopter as seen by the passengers in the train is towards the south-west.

Thus, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: A ship is travelling due east at 10 km/h. A ship 30Â° east of north is always due north from the first ship. The speed of the second ship in km/h is -
question: A ship is travelling due east at 10 km/h. A ship 30Â° east of north is always due north from the first ship. The speed of the second ship in km/h is -
solution:
Let the positive $x$-axis ($+x$) points east ($\hat{\imath}$) and the positive $y$-axis ($+y$) points north ($\hat{\jmath}$).

The first ship is traveling due east at a speed of 10 km/h.

$$\vec{v}_1 = 10\hat{\imath}$$

The second ship moves with an unknown speed $v_2$ at a heading of $30^\circ$ East of North.

The eastward component is $v_{2x} = v_2 \sin(30^\circ)$

The northward component $v_{2y} = v_2 \cos(30^\circ)$

Thus, the velocity vector of the second ship is:

$$\vec{v}_2 = v_2 \sin(30^\circ)\,\hat{\imath} + v_2 \cos(30^\circ)\,\hat{\jmath}$$

$$\vec{v}_2 = \frac{v_2}{2}\hat{\imath} + \frac{\sqrt{3}v_2}{2}\hat{\jmath}$$

The relative velocity of the second ship with respect to the first ship,

$$\vec{v}_{2/1} = \vec{v}_2 - \vec{v}_1$$

$$\vec{v}_{2/1} = \left( \frac{v_2}{2}\hat{i} + \frac{\sqrt{3}v_2}{2}\hat{j} \right) - (10\hat{i})$$

$$\vec{v}_{2/1} = \left( \frac{v_2}{2} - 10 \right)\hat{i} + \left( \frac{\sqrt{3}v_2}{2} \right)\hat{j}$$

It is given that the second ship is always due north from the first ship, so the horizontal component of this relative velocity vector must be zero:

$$\frac{v_2}{2} - 10 = 0$$

$$\frac{v_2}{2} = 10$$

$$v_2 = 20\ \text{km/h}$$

Therefore, the speed of the second ship is $20\ \text{km/h}$. Thus, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: For four particles A, B, C & D the velocities of one with respect to other are given as $\vec{v}_{DC}$ is 20 m/s towards north, $\vec{v}_{BC}$ is 20 m/s towards east and $\vec{v}_{BA}$ is 20 m/s towards south. Then $\vec{v}_{DA}$ is
question: For four particles A, B, C & D the velocities of one with respect to other are given as $\vec{v}_{DC}$ is 20 m/s towards north, $\vec{v}_{BC}$ is 20 m/s towards east and $\vec{v}_{BA}$ is 20 m/s towards south. Then $\vec{v}_{DA}$ is
solution:
$\vec{v}_{DC}$ is 20 m/s towards North, $\vec{v}_{DC} = 20\hat{\jmath}$

$\vec{v}_{BC}$ is 20 m/s towards East, $\vec{v}_{BC} = 20\hat{\imath}$

$\vec{v}_{BA}$ is 20 m/s towards South, $\vec{v}_{BA} = -20\hat{\jmath}$

The relative velocity of particle D with respect to C is $\vec{v}_{DC} = \vec{v}_D - \vec{v}_C = 20\hat{\jmath}$

The relative velocity of particle B with respect to C is $\vec{v}_{BC} = \vec{v}_B - \vec{v}_C = 20\hat{\imath}$

The relative velocity of particle B with respect to A is $\vec{v}_{BA} = \vec{v}_B - \vec{v}_A = -20\hat{\jmath}$

The relative velocity vector of particle D with respect to particle A,

$$\vec{v}_{DA} = \vec{v}_D - \vec{v}_A$$

$$\vec{v}_{DA} = \vec{v}_D - \vec{v}_C + \vec{v}_C - \vec{v}_B + \vec{v}_B - \vec{v}_A$$

$$\vec{v}_{DA} = (\vec{v}_D - \vec{v}_C) - (\vec{v}_B - \vec{v}_C) + (\vec{v}_B - \vec{v}_A)$$

$$\vec{v}_{DA} = \vec{v}_{DC} - \vec{v}_{BC} + \vec{v}_{BA}$$

Substituting the expressions,

$$\vec{v}_{DA} = (20\hat{\jmath}) - (20\hat{\imath}) + (-20\hat{\jmath})$$

$$\vec{v}_{DA} = 20\hat{\jmath} - 20\hat{\imath} - 20\hat{\jmath}$$

$$\vec{v}_{DA} = -20\hat{\imath}\ \text{m/s}$$

The negative sign shows that it is along the negative x-axis, which is west direction. The magnitude of this vector is 20 m/s. Therefore, the relative velocity $\vec{v}_{DA}$ is 20 m/s towards west.

Thus, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: Two persons P and Q start from points A and B respectively as shown in figure. P and Q have speed $v = 12\,\text{m/s}$ in shown directions towards point O. When the distance between P and Q is $120\,\text{m}$, then Q increases its speed to $15\,\text{m/s}$. Then find out who will reach the point O first.
question: Two persons P and Q start from points A and B respectively as shown in figure. P and Q have speed $v = 12\,\text{m/s}$ in shown directions towards point O. When the distance between P and Q is $120\,\text{m}$, then Q increases its speed to $15\,\text{m/s}$. Then find out who will reach the point O first.
solution:
Point $A$ is the starting point of person $P$. Point $B$ is the starting point of person $Q$.

Both the persons are heading towards the target point is O .

The horizontal distance from A to O is 240 m . Thus, P needs to cover a total distance of $\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{P}}=240 \mathrm{~m}$ along the horizontal line to reach O .

The vertical distance between the line AO and point B is 180 m .

Point Q starts at B and moves directly along the line BO towards O .

The total direct distance BO using the Pythagorean theorem is,

$$ \mathrm{BO}=\sqrt{240^2+180^2}=\sqrt{57600+32400}=\sqrt{90000}=300 \mathrm{~m} $$

Both P and Q start at the same time with an initial speed of $\mathrm{v}=12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

Let O is at the origin $(0,0)$ :

Position of P at time t is $\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{P}}(\mathrm{t})=240-12 \mathrm{t}, \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{P}}(\mathrm{t})=0$

Q moves along the line BO towards $(0,0)$.

The angle with the horizontal is $37^{\circ}$, so:

Horizontal component of Q's velocity is $v_x = 12\cos(37^\circ) = 12 \times \frac{4}{5} = 9.6\ \mathrm{m/s}$

Vertical component of Q's velocity is $v_y = 12\sin(37^\circ) = 12 \times \frac{3}{5} = 7.2\ \mathrm{m/s}$

Thus, the position of Q at time t is:

$$x_Q(t) = 240 - 9.6t$$

$$y_Q(t) = -180 + 7.2t$$

The distance squared between P and Q at any time t is :

$$\Delta x = x_P(t) - x_Q(t) = (240 - 12t) - (240 - 9.6t) = -2.4t$$

$$\Delta y = y_P(t) - y_Q(t) = 0 - (-180 + 7.2t) = 180 - 7.2t$$

It is given that the distance $d = 120\ \mathrm{m}$:

$$d^2 = (\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2$$

$$120^2 = (-2.4t)^2 + (180 - 7.2t)^2$$

$$14400 = 5.76t^2 + 32400 - 2592t + 51.84t^2$$

$$57.6t^2 - 2592t + 18000 = 0$$

$$t^2 - 45t + 312.5 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$t = \frac{45 \pm \sqrt{(-45)^2 - 4 \times 1 \times 312.5}}{2}$$

$$t = \frac{45 \pm \sqrt{2025 - 1250}}{2} = \frac{45 \pm \sqrt{775}}{2}$$

$$t_1 = \frac{45 - 27.84}{2} \approx 8.58\ \mathrm{s}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{45 + 27.84}{2} \approx 36.42\ \mathrm{s}$$

The event first happens at $t_1 \approx 8.58\ \mathrm{s}$.

Person P doesn't change speed. Their speed remains $12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ for the entire journey.

Total time taken by $\mathrm{P}=\frac{\text { Total distance }(\mathrm{AO})}{\text { speed }}=\frac{240}{12}=20 \mathrm{~s}$

For person Q distance covered in time up to $\mathrm{t}_1=8.58 \mathrm{~s}$ is,

$$ \mathrm{d}_1=\mathrm{v} \times \mathrm{t}_1=12 \times 8.58=102.96 \mathrm{~m} $$

Remaining distance to point O is,

$$ \mathrm{d}_{\text {remaining }}=300-102.96=197.04 \mathrm{~m} $$

Time taken to cover the remaining distance at the new speed $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is,

$$ \mathrm{t}_{\text {remaining }}=\frac{197.04}{15} \approx 13.14 \mathrm{~s} $$

Total time taken by $\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{t}_1+\mathrm{t}_{\text {remaining }}=8.58+13.14=21.72 \mathrm{~s}$

Total time taken by $\mathrm{P}=20 \mathrm{~s}$

Total time taken by $\mathrm{Q}=21.72 \mathrm{~s}$

Hence, P reaches point O first. Therefore, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: Two aeroplanes fly from their respective positions 'A' and 'B' starting at the same time and reach the point 'C' simultaneously when wind was not blowing. On a windy day they head towards 'C' but both reach the point 'D' simultaneously in the same time which they took to reach 'C'. Then the wind is blowing in
question: Two aeroplanes fly from their respective positions 'A' and 'B' starting at the same time and reach the point 'C' simultaneously when wind was not blowing. On a windy day they head towards 'C' but both reach the point 'D' simultaneously in the same time which they took to reach 'C'. Then the wind is blowing in
options: Direction making an angle $0 < \theta < 90$ with North towards East.
solution:
Let $\vec{r}_A$, $\vec{r}_B$, $\vec{r}_C$, and $\vec{r}_D$ represent the position coordinates of locations A, B, C, and D respectively.

Without wind the two aeroplanes start simultaneously from positions A and B and arrive at position C at the exact same instant of time $t$.

The displacement vector for the first plane is $\vec{s}_1 = \vec{r}_C - \vec{r}_A$

The displacement vector for the second plane is $\vec{s}_2 = \vec{r}_C - \vec{r}_B$

Let the constant velocities of the two aeroplanes relative to the air mass be $\vec{v}_1$ and $\vec{v}_2$. Since there is no wind, their ground velocities are equal to their airspeed velocities:

$$\vec{s}_1 = \vec{v}_1\, t \Rightarrow \vec{r}_C - \vec{r}_A = \vec{v}_1\, t$$

$$\vec{s}_2 = \vec{v}_2\, t \Rightarrow \vec{r}_C - \vec{r}_B = \vec{v}_2\, t$$

On the windy day, both airplanes head directly towards point C with their original engine speed. This means their airspeed velocity vectors relative to the medium remain completely unchanged ($\vec{v}_1$ and $\vec{v}_2$).

A uniform wind velocity vector $\vec{v}_w$ blows.

The resultant ground velocity ($\vec{v}'$) of any object moving through a flowing medium is the vector sum of its

velocity relative to the medium and the velocity of the medium itself:

$$\vec{v}' = \vec{v}_{\text{plane/air}} + \vec{v}_{\text{wind}}$$

Ground velocity of the first plane is $\vec{v}'_1 = \vec{v}_1 + \vec{v}_w$

Ground velocity of the second plane is $\vec{v}'_2 = \vec{v}_2 + \vec{v}_w$

For the same time interval $t$ and both arrive simultaneously at a new location D.

Their new ground displacements after time $t$ as:

For plane A,

$$\vec{r}_D - \vec{r}_A = \vec{v}'_1 t = (\vec{v}_1 + \vec{v}_w)t = \vec{v}_1 t + \vec{v}_w t$$

$$\vec{r}_D - \vec{r}_A = (\vec{r}_C - \vec{r}_A) + \vec{v}_w t$$

$$\vec{r}_D = \vec{r}_C + \vec{v}_w t$$

$$\vec{r}_D - \vec{r}_C = \vec{v}_w t$$

So, the direction of the wind vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{w}}$ is from C to D .

Therefore, the wind is blowing in the north-west direction.

Thus, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: A man walking eastward at $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ observes that wind is blowing from the north. On doubling his speed eastward, he observes that wind is blowing from north-east. Find the velocity of the wind.
question: A man walking eastward at $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ observes that wind is blowing from the north. On doubling his speed eastward, he observes that wind is blowing from north-east. Find the velocity of the wind.
solution:
Let the velocity of wind be

$$ v_w = (v_1 \hat{i} + v_2 \hat{j}) \ \mathrm{m/s} $$

The man walks eastward, so his velocity is

$$ v_m = 5 \hat{i} $$

The velocity of wind as seen by the man (relative velocity) is

$$ v_{wm} = v_w - v_m = (v_1 - 5)\hat{i} + v_2 \hat{j} $$

In the first condition, the man feels the wind coming directly from the north. This means the relative wind is along the south direction only, so its eastâ€“west component is zero.

$$ v_1 - 5 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = 5 \ \mathrm{m/s} $$

Now, in the second condition, the man doubles his speed to $10 \ \mathrm{m/s}$ eastward. The wind now seems to come from the north-east direction, which means the angle between the north direction and the apparent wind is $45^\circ$.

For this case,

$$ \tan 45^\circ = \frac{v_2}{v_1 - 10} $$

Since $\tan 45^\circ = 1$, we have

$$ v_2 = v_1 - 10 $$

Putting $v_1 = 5$,

$$ v_2 = 5 - 10 = -5 \ \mathrm{m/s} $$

Hence, the velocity of wind is

$$ v_w = (5\hat{i} - 5\hat{j}) \ \mathrm{m/s} $$

subject: Physics
q: The relative velocity of a car 'A' with respect to car B is $30\sqrt{2}$ m/s due North-East. The velocity of car 'B' is 20 m/s due south. The relative velocity of car 'C' with respect to car 'A' is $10\sqrt{2}$ m/s due North-West. The speed of car C and the direction (in terms of angle it makes with the east) are
question: The relative velocity of a car 'A' with respect to car B is $30\sqrt{2}$ m/s due North-East. The velocity of car 'B' is 20 m/s due south. The relative velocity of car 'C' with respect to car 'A' is $10\sqrt{2}$ m/s due North-West. The speed of car C and the direction (in terms of angle it makes with the east) are
solution:
The velocity of car B is given as $20$ m/s due South. So, we can write it as:

$$ \vec{v}_B = -20\hat{j} \text{ m/s} $$

The relative velocity of car A with respect to car B, denoted as $\vec{v}_{AB}$, is $30\sqrt{2}$ m/s due North-East. The North-East direction makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with both the North and East axes. We can resolve this into components:

$$ \vec{v}_{AB} = 30\sqrt{2}(\cos 45^{\circ} \hat{i} + \sin 45^{\circ} \hat{j}) $$

Since $\cos 45^{\circ} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\sin 45^{\circ} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, this simplifies to:

$$ \vec{v}_{AB} = 30\hat{i} + 30\hat{j} \text{ m/s} $$

We know that relative velocity is defined as $\vec{v}_{AB} = \vec{v}_A - \vec{v}_B$. We can rearrange this to find the actual velocity of car A:

$$ \vec{v}_A = \vec{v}_{AB} + \vec{v}_B $$

Substitute the known values into the equation:

$$ \vec{v}_A = (30\hat{i} + 30\hat{j}) + (-20\hat{j}) $$

$$ \vec{v}_A = 30\hat{i} + 10\hat{j} \text{ m/s} $$

Next, we are given the relative velocity of car C with respect to car A, denoted as $\vec{v}_{CA}$, which is $10\sqrt{2}$ m/s due North-West. The North-West direction points between West ($-\hat{i}$) and North ($\hat{j}$), making an angle of $45^\circ$ with both. Finding its components:

$$ \vec{v}_{CA} = 10\sqrt{2}(-\cos 45^{\circ} \hat{i} + \sin 45^{\circ} \hat{j}) $$

$$ \vec{v}_{CA} = -10\hat{i} + 10\hat{j} \text{ m/s} $$

Similarly, using the relative velocity formula $\vec{v}_{CA} = \vec{v}_C - \vec{v}_A$, we can find the velocity of car C:

$$ \vec{v}_C = \vec{v}_{CA} + \vec{v}_A $$

Substitute the values we found:

$$ \vec{v}_C = (-10\hat{i} + 10\hat{j}) + (30\hat{i} + 10\hat{j}) $$

$$ \vec{v}_C = 20\hat{i} + 20\hat{j} \text{ m/s} $$

Now, we just need to find the speed (magnitude) and direction of car C.

The speed of car C is the magnitude of the velocity vector:

$$ |\vec{v}_C| = \sqrt{(20)^2 + (20)^2} $$

$$ |\vec{v}_C| = \sqrt{400 + 400} = \sqrt{800} $$

$$ |\vec{v}_C| = 20\sqrt{2} \text{ m/s} $$

To find the direction, let $\theta$ be the angle of the velocity vector with the East direction (the positive x-axis). We use the tangent ratio:

$$ \tan \theta = \frac{v_y}{v_x} = \frac{20}{20} = 1 $$

This gives $\theta = 45^{\circ}$. Since both the x and y components are positive, the direction makes a $45^{\circ}$ angle strictly with the East.

The correct choice is Option A.

subject: Physics
q: A bird is moving northward with speed 40 km hâ€“1. A train is moving eastward with speed 40 km hâ€“1. Find the velocity of the bird as observed by a person sitting in the train.
question: A bird is moving northward with speed 40 km hâ€“1. A train is moving eastward with speed 40 km hâ€“1. Find the velocity of the bird as observed by a person sitting in the train.
solution:
The bird moves towards the north with speed $40 \text{ km h}^{-1}$, and the train moves towards the east with the same speed $40 \text{ km h}^{-1}$. To get the birdâ€™s velocity as seen from the train, we imagine that we apply an extra velocity equal to $-\vec{V}_T$ (opposite to the trainâ€™s velocity) to both the train and the bird.

After doing that, the train becomes at rest. But the bird now has two velocity components â€” one towards the north ($\vec{V}_B$) and another towards the west ($-\vec{V}_T$), both of the same magnitude, $40 \text{ km h}^{-1}$.

The angle between these two velocity directions is $90^\circ$, so the relative velocity can be found using the Pythagoras theorem:

$$ \left| \vec{V}_{BT} \right| = \sqrt{ \left| \vec{V}_B \right|^2 + \left| -\vec{V}_T \right|^2 } = \sqrt{ 40^2 + 40^2 } = 40 \sqrt{2} \text{ km h}^{-1} $$

Hence, the bird appears to move towards the north-west direction with a speed of $40\sqrt{2} \text{ km h}^{-1}$ as seen from the train.

subject: Physics
q: Airplanes $A$ and $B$ are flying with constant velocity in the same vertical plane respectively at angle $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with respect to the horizontal as shown in the figure. The speed of $A$ is $100 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. At time $t=$ 0 s , an observer in $A$ finds $B$ at a distance of 500 m . This observer sees $B$ moving with a constant velocity perpendicular to the line of motion of $A$. If at $t=t_0$, $A$ just escapes being hit by $B$, then $t_0$ in seconds is
question: Airplanes $A$ and $B$ are flying with constant velocity in the same vertical plane respectively at angle $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with respect to the horizontal as shown in the figure. The speed of $A$ is $100 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. At time $t=$ 0 s , an observer in $A$ finds $B$ at a distance of 500 m . This observer sees $B$ moving with a constant velocity perpendicular to the line of motion of $A$. If at $t=t_0$, $A$ just escapes being hit by $B$, then $t_0$ in seconds is
solution:
Since the observer is in airplane A , so the motion of B relative to $\mathrm{A}\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{BA}}\right)$.

For Airplane A :

The speed is $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}}=100 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at $30^{\circ}$

So, the velocity of A is,

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{A}}=\left(100 \sqrt{3} \cos 30^{\circ}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(100 \sqrt{3} \sin 30^{\circ}\right) \hat{\jmath} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\vec{v}_A=\left(100 \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(100 \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\right) \hat{\jmath}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \vec{v}_A=150 \hat{\imath}+50 \sqrt{3}\hat{\jmath} $$

For Airplane B:

Let the speed of B be $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}$ at $60^{\circ}$, then velocity vector of B is ;

$$ \vec{v}_B=\left(v_B \cos 60^{\circ}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(v_B \sin 60^{\circ}\right) \hat{\jmath} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \vec{v}_B=\left(\frac{v_B}{2}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(\frac{v_B \sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \hat{\jmath} $$

So, the relative velocity of B with respect to A is,

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{BA}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{B}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{A}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{BA}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}}{2}-150\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}} \sqrt{3}}{2}-50 \sqrt{3}\right) \hat{\jmath}$

It is given that the observer in A sees B moving perpendicular to A's path. If two vectors are perpendicular, their dot product is zero.

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{BA}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{A}}=0 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\left[\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}}{2}-150\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}} \sqrt{3}}{2}-50 \sqrt{3}\right) \hat{\jmath}\right] \cdot[150 \hat{\imath}+50 \sqrt{3} \hat{\jmath}]=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $150\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}}{2}-150\right)+50 \sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}} \sqrt{3}}{2}-50 \sqrt{3}\right)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $75 v_B-22500+50(3)\left(\frac{v_B}{2}-50\right)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $75 v_B-22500+150\left(\frac{v_B}{2}-50\right)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $75 v_B-22500+75 v_B-7500=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ 150 \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=30000 \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=200 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

Substituting $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=200$ into the relative velocity components:

$$ \begin{aligned} & x \text {-component }=\frac{200}{2}-150=-50 \\ & y \text {-component }=\frac{200 \sqrt{3}}{2}-50 \sqrt{3}=50 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned} $$

So, the magnitude of the relative velocity is;

$$ \left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{BA}}\right|=\sqrt{(-50)^2+(50 \sqrt{3})^2} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{BA}}\right|=\sqrt{2500+7500}=\sqrt{10000}=100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

The hit or escape occurs based on the relative distance. The initial relative distance is $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{BA}}=500 \mathrm{~m}$. For A to just escape being hit, B must travel the component of this distance along the relative velocity path.

$$ \mathrm{t}_0=\frac{\text { Relative Distance }}{\text { Relative speed }} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ t_0=\frac{500}{100}=5 \text { seconds } $$

Therefore, the time $\mathbf{t}_{\mathbf{0}}$ is $\mathbf{5}$ seconds.

subject: Physics
q: A particle is projected at angle $60^{\circ}$ with speed $10 \sqrt{3}$, from the point ' $A$ ' as shown in the fig. At the same time the wedge is made to move with speed $10 \sqrt{3}$ towards right as shown in the figure. If the time after which particle strikes with wedge is $\frac{\mathrm{N}}{13}$ seconds, then find out the value of $\mathrm{N} .\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^2\right)$
question: A particle is projected at angle $60^{\circ}$ with speed $10 \sqrt{3}$, from the point ' $A$ ' as shown in the fig. At the same time the wedge is made to move with speed $10 \sqrt{3}$ towards right as shown in the figure. If the time after which particle strikes with wedge is $\frac{\mathrm{N}}{13}$ seconds, then find out the value of $\mathrm{N} .\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^2\right)$
solution:
Let the horizontal direction towards the right be the positive x -axis $(+\mathrm{x})$ and the vertical direction upwards be the positive y -axis $(+\mathrm{y})$.

On resolving the initial velocity vector of the particle projected from point A at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ to the horizontal:

Horizontal component is $u_{px} = u_p \cos(60^{\circ}) = 10\sqrt{3} \times \frac{1}{2} = 5\sqrt{3}\ \mathrm{m/s}$

Vertical component is $u_{py} = u_p \sin(60^{\circ}) = 10\sqrt{3} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 15\ \mathrm{m/s}$

Thus, the velocity vector of the particle at any time $t$ before collision, moving under gravity $(a_x = 0, a_y = -g = -10\ \mathrm{m/s^2})$, is:

$$\vec{v}_p(t) = 5\sqrt{3}\hat{i} + (15 - gt)\hat{j} = 5\sqrt{3}\hat{i} + (15 - 10t)\hat{j}$$

The wedge moves horizontally to the right with a constant speed of $10\sqrt{3}\ \mathrm{m/s}$. Its velocity vector is:

$$\vec{v}_w = 10\sqrt{3}\hat{i} + 0\hat{j}$$

The relative initial velocity of the particle with respect to the wedge is:

$$\vec{u}_{p/w} = \vec{u}_p - \vec{v}_w$$

$$\vec{u}_{p/w} = (5\sqrt{3}\hat{i} + 15\hat{j}) - (10\sqrt{3}\hat{i}) = -5\sqrt{3}\hat{i} + 15\hat{j}$$

Acceleration of wedge is $\vec{a}_w = 0$.

The relative acceleration of the particle with respect to the wedge is

$$\vec{a}_{p/w} = \vec{a}_p - \vec{a}_w = -10\hat{j} - 0 = -10\hat{j}$$

The inclined face of the wedge forms an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal.

The slope $(m)$ of this inclined surface line is:

$$m = \tan(180^{\circ} - 30^{\circ}) = -\tan(30^{\circ}) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Let the point A is origin $A(0,0)$. Since it passes through the origin $(0,0)$ in the wedge's frame, the equation of the inclined surface plane is:

$$y = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}x \Rightarrow x = -\sqrt{3}y$$

The particle strikes the wedge when its relative position coordinates $(x_{\mathrm{rel}}, y_{\mathrm{rel}})$ satisfy this surface line equation.

Using the kinematic equation $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$:

$$x_{\mathrm{rel}}(t) = u_{\mathrm{rel},x} t + \frac{1}{2} a_{\mathrm{rel},x} t^2 = -5\sqrt{3}t + 0 = -5\sqrt{3}t$$

$$y_{\mathrm{rel}}(t) = u_{\mathrm{rel},y} t + \frac{1}{2} a_{\mathrm{rel},y} t^2 = 15t - \frac{1}{2}(10)t^2 = 15t - 5t^2$$

Substituting into the surface equation $x_{\mathrm{rel}} = -\sqrt{3}y_{\mathrm{rel}}$:

$$-5\sqrt{3}t = -\sqrt{3}(15t - 5t^2)$$

$$5t = 15t - 5t^2$$

$$5t^2 - 10t = 0$$

$$5t(t - 2) = 0$$

$$t = 2\ \text{seconds}$$

It is given that the impact time is equal to $\frac{\mathrm{N}}{13}$ seconds.

$$ \frac{N}{13}=2 \Rightarrow N=2 \times 13=26 $$

Therefore, the value of N is 26 .

subject: Physics
q: CE and DF are two walls of equal height ( 20 meter) from which two particles $A$ and $B$ of same mass are projected as shown in the figure. A is projected horizontally towards left while B is projected at an angle $37^{\circ}$ (with horizontal towards left) with velocity $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. If $A$ always sees $B$ to be moving perpendicular to $E F$, then find out the range of $A$ on ground in meter.
question: CE and DF are two walls of equal height ( 20 meter) from which two particles $A$ and $B$ of same mass are projected as shown in the figure. A is projected horizontally towards left while B is projected at an angle $37^{\circ}$ (with horizontal towards left) with velocity $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. If $A$ always sees $B$ to be moving perpendicular to $E F$, then find out the range of $A$ on ground in meter.
solution:
Particle B is projected from the right wall DF towards the left at an angle of $37^{\circ}$ with the horizontal, with a speed of $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. Its initial velocity vector in component form is,

Horizontal component is $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{Bx}}=-15 \cos \left(37^{\circ}\right)=-15 \times \frac{4}{5}=-12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$

Vertical component is $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{By}}=+15 \sin \left(37^{\circ}\right)=15 \times \frac{3}{5}=+9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$

Thus, the initial velocity vector of $B$ is $\vec{u}_B=-12 \hat{\imath}+9 \hat{\jmath}$

Since both particles move freely under gravity, they have the same downward acceleration,

$$\vec{a}_A = \vec{a}_B = -g\hat{j} = -10\hat{j}$$

The velocity of particle B as function of time using $v = u + at$ is,

$$\vec{v}_B(t) = -12\hat{i} + (9 - gt)\hat{j}$$

Particle A is projected horizontally towards the left from the top of the left wall CE.

Let its unknown initial speed be $v_A$. Its velocity vector at any time $t$ is:

$$\vec{v}_A(t) = -v_A\hat{i} + (0 - gt)\hat{j} = -v_A\hat{i} - gt\hat{j}$$

It is given that A always sees B to be moving perpendicular to EF. So, from the reference frame of observer A, the horizontal component of B's relative velocity must be zero at all times.

The relative velocity vector of particle B with respect to particle A,

$$\vec{v}_{B/A} = \vec{v}_B - \vec{v}_A$$

$$\vec{v}_{B/A} = [-12\hat{i} + (9 - gt)\hat{j}] - [-v_A\hat{i} - gt\hat{j}]$$

$$\vec{v}_{B/A} = (-12 + v_A)\hat{i} + (9 - gt + gt)\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{v}_{B/A} = (v_A - 12)\hat{i} + 9\hat{j}$$

The relative velocity is vertical, so the horizontal $(\hat{i})$ component be zero:

$$v_A - 12 = 0 \Rightarrow v_A = 12\ \text{m/sec}$$

Particle A is a horizontal projectile fired from an initial height $h = 20$ meters with an initial speed $v_A = 12$ m/sec.

Using kinematic equation for vertical displacement $y = y_0 + u_y\ t + \frac{1}{2}a_y\ t^2$,

The time of flight is $t_f$

The vertical displacement is $-h$

The initial vertical velocity $u_{Ay} = 0$ is,

$$0 = h + 0 - \frac{1}{2}gt_f^2 \Rightarrow t_f = \sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}$$

Substituting the value of height $h = 20$ m and $g = 10$ m/sec$^2$:

$$t_f = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20}{10}} = \sqrt{4} = 2\ \text{seconds}$$

The horizontal distance covered by particle A during this time interval is,

$$R = |u_{Ax}| \times t_f = 12 \times 2\ \text{m} = 24\ \text{meters}$$

Therefore, the horizontal range of particle A on the ground is 24 meters.

subject: Physics
q: Ship $A$ moves with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ towards the east. Another ship is moving along a direction $30^{\circ}$ east of north. What must be the speed of the second ship so that, relative to ship $A$, it always stays exactly due north?
question: Ship $A$ moves with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ towards the east. Another ship is moving along a direction $30^{\circ}$ east of north. What must be the speed of the second ship so that, relative to ship $A$, it always stays exactly due north?
solution:
For the second ship to remain always due north of the first ship, their eastward components of velocity must be the same.

Ship $A$ moves due east with speed

$$5\ \text{km h}^{-1}$$

So its whole velocity is eastward.

Now the second ship moves $30^\circ$ east of north.

If its speed is $v$, then its eastward component is

$$v\sin 30^\circ$$

This must be equal to $5$ km/h.

So,

$$v\sin 30^\circ = 5$$

Since

$$\sin 30^\circ = \frac{1}{2}$$

we get

$$v \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 5$$

$$v = 10$$

So the speed of the second ship should be

$$\boxed{10\ \text{km h}^{-1}}$$

subject: Physics
q: Consider two particles, denoted as 1 and 2. Their initial position vectors at time $t=0$ are given as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_1=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_2=-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m}$, while they move with constant velocities $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_1=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_2=b \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. If both particles undergo a collision at $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$, then the numerical value of constant $b$ is:
question: Consider two particles, denoted as 1 and 2. Their initial position vectors at time $t=0$ are given as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_1=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_2=-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m}$, while they move with constant velocities $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_1=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_2=b \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. If both particles undergo a collision at $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$, then the numerical value of constant $b$ is:
solution:
The position of a particle moving with a constant velocity can be found using the simple relation:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{final} = \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{initial} + \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} t $$

Let's find the position of the first particle at $t = 3 \text{ s}$.

Its initial position is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_1 = 5 \hat{\mathrm{i}} + 2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$ and its velocity is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_1 = 4 \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$.

Plugging these values into the equation, we get:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}_1 = (5 \hat{\mathrm{i}} + 2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) + (4 \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \times 3 $$

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}_1 = 5 \hat{\mathrm{i}} + 2 \hat{\mathrm{j}} + 12 \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 9 \hat{\mathrm{j}} $$

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}_1 = 17 \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 7 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \text{ m} $$

Now, let's do the same for the second particle.

Its initial position is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_2 = -4 \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$ and its velocity is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_2 = b \hat{\mathrm{i}} - \hat{\mathrm{j}}$.

The position of the second particle at $t = 3 \text{ s}$ becomes:

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}_2 = (-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) + (b \hat{\mathrm{i}} - \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \times 3 $$

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}_2 = -4 \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} + 3b \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 3 \hat{\mathrm{j}} $$

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}_2 = (-4 + 3b) \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 7 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \text{ m} $$

Because the particles collide at $t = 3 \text{ s}$, their final position vectors must be exactly the same. So, we can equate $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}_1$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}_2$:

$$ 17 \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 7 \hat{\mathrm{j}} = (-4 + 3b) \hat{\mathrm{i}} - 7 \hat{\mathrm{j}} $$

The $\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ components are already identical (both are $-7$). To find $b$, we just need to equate the $\hat{\mathrm{i}}$ components:

$$ 17 = -4 + 3b $$

Bringing $-4$ to the other side gives:

$$ 17 + 4 = 3b $$

$$ 21 = 3b $$

$$ b = \frac{21}{3} $$

$$ b = 7 $$

The value of $b$ is $7$.
